description: Attack path definitions and remediation guidance. Each path describes how an unprivileged user can escalate to Global Administrator-equivalent access through Entra ID misconfigurations.

paths.name: App Owner Escalation
paths.description: A non-admin user owns an app registration that has dangerous application permissions. The user can add a new secret/certificate, authenticate as the app, and inherit its permissions.
chain: User authenticates as app using new credential

detection.method: Cross-reference app owners (non-admins) with apps that have dangerous permissions

remediation: Remove the non-admin user as owner of the app
Replace the dangerous permission with a least-privilege alternative
Apply Conditional Access for workload identities on the service principal
Use Managed Identity instead of app registration where possible

paths.name: Shadow Admin via Service Principal Role
paths.description: A non-admin user owns a service principal that holds a privileged directory role. The user can reset the SP's credentials and act as the SP, inheriting its role.
chain: Service Principal has privileged directory role

detection.method: Cross-reference SP owners (non-admins) with SPs that hold privileged directory roles

remediation: Remove the non-admin user as owner of the service principal
Remove the privileged directory role from the service principal
Use PIM for just-in-time role activation instead of permanent assignments

paths.name: Consent Grant Attack
paths.description: If tenant policy allows user consent, a phishing attack can trick users into granting OAuth consent to a malicious app, giving the attacker access to user data.
chain: Tenant allows user consent for apps
User grants OAuth consent to malicious app
Malicious app accesses user's mail, files, or profile

detection.method: Check tenant authorization policy for user consent settings; enumerate user-consented OAuth2 permission grants

remediation: Set user consent to 'Do not allow user consent' or 'Allow for verified publishers only'
Enable admin consent workflow so users can request access
Review and revoke suspicious user-consented permissions

paths.name: Stale App Credential Exploitation
paths.description: A high-privilege app has not signed in for an extended period but still has valid credentials. An attacker who obtains the credentials can authenticate as the app and exploit its permissions.
chain: App has not signed in for 90+ days
Attacker discovers credentials (repo leak, config file, etc.)

detection.method: Cross-reference high-privilege apps with sign-in activity and credential expiry dates

remediation: Disable or delete the app if no longer needed
Remove the dangerous permissions if the app is still needed
Rotate and then revoke the credentials
Set up alerts for sign-in activity on high-privilege apps

paths.name: Credential Theft via Client Secret
paths.description: A high-privilege app uses a client secret (plain text string) rather than a certificate or managed identity. Secrets are easier to exfiltrate from code repos, config files, or environment variables.
chain: App uses client secret (not certificate or managed identity)
Secret stored in code repo, config file, Key Vault, or environment variable
Attacker gains access to secret storage
Attacker authenticates as app using secret

detection.method: Check credential types on high-privilege apps; flag password credentials over certificates or federated credentials

remediation: Migrate from client secret to certificate authentication
Use Managed Identity where the app runs in Azure
Use Federated Identity Credentials for external workloads (GitHub Actions, etc.)
If secrets are required, store them in Azure Key Vault with access policies
